title: Corporates revolt over AI bill shock
full_text:
Token trap The rise of agentic tools is causing financial headaches for companies as they try to rein in staff usage, writes Paul Smith.
As Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller works away on his laptop, there is a new addition to his home screen to remind him not to get carried away with his use of artificial intelligence.
An AI token tracker gives him a running tally of how many tokens he is using with his questions to chatbots, strategy simulations and other AI agent tasks. He has told colleagues it is about keeping the fact that each interaction costs money at the front of his mind, so he can judge if Westpac is getting a return on its investment.
Like most of his peers, Miller is publicly all-in on AI. When Microsoft boss Satya Nadella visited Australia in April on a global company "AI tour", Miller was a guest speaker at the conference talking about the bank's plans for its use of the technology.
While Miller is enthusiastic about AI, he is also alive to a risk of bill shock from its use, which is increasingly a concern for companies and individuals.
"We want all of our people to be engaged and empowered by AI, not constrained," says Dan Jermyn, Westpac's chief AI officer. "At the same time, it's increasingly important that we use the right tool for the job, given the growing cost of token use. We're still in the early phases, but we think we're getting that balance right at this point."
The cost of AI tokens is why there has been a boom in demand for cheaper Chinese AI models, which in turn is driving a price war between United States giants OpenAI and Anthropic. The price war is threatening to undermine those American companies' business models and valuations ahead of planning public market listings.
Companies pay for their employees' AI use by the token. These are the currency of the AI age and are split into input tokens - based on how long a prompt/question is, and how much detail it includes - and more expensive output tokens, where longer answers and more complicated actions use more tokens.
Firms ranging from Amazon and Walmart to Cisco, Uber and Meta have already discovered that, once employees started following instructions to use as much AI as possible, it can quickly blow up the budget.
Uber's chief technology officer admitted in April that uncontrolled AI use saw it use up its annual AI budget in just three months.
The rise of agentic AI, where AI bots start researching and vibe coding by themselves, has only made matters worse as companies try to rein in costs.
The outcome is that the industry is now at a point where efficiency and cheaper prices are more important to many customers than an AI model's position on capability leaderboards.
Uncomfortably for OpenAI and Anthropic, their Chinese rivals such as DeepSeek, Z.ai (formerly Zhipu AI), Alibaba and Baidu, are dramatically undercutting them on price, forcing them to focus on smaller, stripped back models that use fewer tokens.
The tension was highlighted last week when Anthropic accused Alibaba of trying to steal details of its models to improve their own. Then, The Wall Street Journal reported that Z.ai has matched the performance of Anthropic's powerful model Mythos in hunting some cybersecurity bugs.
Earlier this month, the WSJ also reported that both OpenAI and Anthropic are planning major price cuts to try and nail down corporate market share as the pair both race towards public listings. How low they can go, while also swallowing huge losses on hundreds of billions invested in infrastructure, will soon be a question for the public markets to answer.
"The model companies have borrowed a lot of pages from the books of the watch industry so far, in convincing people that it is worth paying a premium for the very best, and some companies do want to stay on the leading edge of new improvements," says Gartner AI analyst Arun Chandrasekaran.
"But there is still a fact that a $100 watch tells the same time as a $100,000 watch and there are plenty of times when small language models and open weights models are good enough."
OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI - established by Elon Musk - and Google models do not offer open-weights versions, only Meta and other big US firms such as Amazon and Microsoft, have adopted that approach.
An open-weights model is one that can be downloaded and run on a company's own servers, rather than having all the work done in data centres run by AI companies or cloud computing providers.
The Chinese model makers do have open-weights models, however, and that is how they are finding their way into the operations of Australian and other Western organisations.
AI price comparisons are difficult because there are so many different models from each provider that can be used to perform a task. However, comparing the token costs of the companies' flagship models shows the disparity that has OpenAI and Anthropic worried.
In May, DeepSeek announced that a 75 per cent discount on its token prices would be retained permanently. DeepSeek V4-Pro output tokens cost just 87¢ per million, compared with OpenAI's GPT-5.5, which cost $US30 ($45.54) per million.
Anthropic's Claude 4.8 Opus is a bit cheaper at $US25 per million but its Fable 5 and Mythos models, which were taken off the market due to the US government's export controls earlier this month, were selling for $US50 per million.
"The cheapest models are Chinese," says Will Liang the founder of Australian AI advisory firm Amplify AI. "They are powerful - at a fraction of the price - and, as open weights, capable of being hosted in the US or even locally in Australia.
"The new [Z.ai] GLM 5.2, for instance, has topped Gemini and beaten GPT and Claude across multiple benchmarks.
"What remains unresolved is the regulatory question: for regulated industries, there's real confusion about whether Chinese models can be used at all, so most firms steer clear regardless."
Most corporate AI users access models through the cloud computing services of their providers such as Microsoft Azure, Amazon's Bedrock and Google's Vertex AI, or through software-as-a-service intermediaries like Salesforce, Atlassian and ServiceNow.
These platforms will often select which model is best suited to a task, meaning an end customer has little brand loyalty to any particular AI model maker, making them akin to utilities.
Steve Hind, the co-founder and chief executive of $200 million valued Sydney AI start-up Lorikeet, says he has often been able to find open-weights models that cost 80 per cent to 90 per cent less than closed-weights models from Anthropic and OpenAI - while offering the same performance.
He doesn't care which lab made the models and instead prefers whichever is most effective for the task at hand. The perceived risk of dealing with Chinese-owned models is mitigated by running them on equipment outside of China on infrastructure run by providers that meet Australian cybersecurity standards.
"All open-weights models - including those trained by Chinese labs - have training that is inspectable, and all of our inference [AI output] on those models is run in the US or Australia," Hind says. "So regardless of which lab developed the model the data is managed and stored in accordance with our standards."
Similarly, Ben Thompson, the co-founder and chief executive of $2 billion-valued software company Employment Hero, has spoken about how the use of vibe coding platforms such as Anthropic's Claude Code have dramatically changed the way his company operates and for the better.
However, he admits it is an ongoing challenge trying to figure out how to let staff experiment with AI without blowing the budget.
"We've gone too hard in both directions at different points, but we've landed in a reasonable place," Thompson says. "All staff get a base set of licenses for tools like Gemini, and anything more niche goes through an approval process.
"The key insight for us has been that not every task needs the most powerful model. You don't need Claude Opus to edit an email. Not everyone needs Claude Code. The real discipline is matching the complexity of the task to the right tool and training your team to think that way too.
"We're not trying to pick a single winner and lock in. Every major provider is shipping upgraded models at an extraordinary pace, so boxing yourself into one provider for a marginal cost-saving feels like the wrong trade-off. We'd rather use the best tool for the job across a broad range of options."
Mohit Sharma, the managing director of Mindfields, which is an independent adviser to companies using AI, says organisations will have to increasingly guard against the commercial instincts of the big tech players as they try to turn their huge AI investments into profits.
He says a lack of transparency about how many tokens are being used is the biggest threat to any company successfully adopting AI.
"Ask a typical business sponsor of an AI initiative to explain what a token is, or how many their platform is consuming, and you will often be met by silence," Sharma says.
"That silence is expensive. Token consumption is not linear; it compounds in ways that are counterintuitive and commercially significant."
While this may appear gloomy for OpenAI and Anthropic's prospects, Chandrasekaran says demand from companies around the world is such that they will not have to drop their prices too dramatically in the run-up to their expected publiclistings.
OpenAI and Anthropic will increasingly offer more evolved services, which threaten the products of software and services firms, he says.
In research published in May, Goldman Sachs said global token consumption would explode 22-fold by 2030 and 55-fold by 2040.
"In the near-term, the sheer demand for their models and their commercial products is just sky high, when compared to their ability to supply it," says Chandrasekaran.
Commonwealth Bank chief AI officer Ranil Boteju suggests this reading of the situation is right, and that AI use is going to continue to increase.
However, even though Australia's biggest mortgage lender has positioned itself as an AI leader, this enthusiasm is being kept in check by new controls on how much everyone uses - just like at its rival Westpac led by Miller.
"We have tracking in place to review token usage and forecast based on upcoming use cases and projected ongoing costs ... and can apply usage caps where appropriate," says Boteju.
"We are also scaling our use of small language models and smaller open-source models. As AI use continues to scale, organisations will need to be disciplined about prioritising tokens towards the use cases and teams where they can deliver the best outcomes."AFR
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Smith
heading: Data Science and AI
